prompt:
Our reporting pipeline pulls from a data platform that exposes server-managed cursors. Every team iterates the feed differently and we keep losing rows when a cursor expires mid-job (we restart from the beginning and emit duplicates into the output). I need cursorfeed.py, stdlib only. test_cursorfeed.py is the acceptance contract; `python3 test_cursorfeed.py` green means done; test file read-only.

Note: go-pagewalk (existing seed) is about pagination FORMATS (Link headers, body cursors); go-syncdelta is about sync checkpointing. This seed is the SERVER-CURSOR LIFECYCLE — the server allocates and tracks a cursor object; the client manages its state machine across open/expired/closed transitions.

The server API:
  POST /cursors                       → 201 { "cursor_id": "...", "key": null }
  GET  /cursors/<id>?limit=N[&seek_after=<key>]  → 200 { "rows": [...], "next_key": <str|null> }
                                        or 410 { "error": "cursor_expired" }
  DELETE /cursors/<id>                → 204

Rows: objects with a "key" field (string) used for deduplication and seek.

CursorState: an enum (from enum import Enum) with three members — OPEN (cursor allocated and active on the server), EXPIRED (server returned 410), CLOSED (no active cursor).

class CursorFeed(base_url, *, page_size=100)
  state property → CursorState (starts CLOSED)

  open() — POST /cursors to allocate a new cursor; transition to OPEN.
  close() — if OPEN or EXPIRED, DELETE /cursors/<id>; transition to CLOSED. If already CLOSED, return immediately with no network call.
  iterate() → generator that yields rows (dicts) one at a time:
    1. Call open() if not already OPEN.
    2. Fetch pages via GET /cursors/<id>?limit=<page_size>, yield each row.
    3. If a page returns 410 (cursor expired): reopen (open a new cursor), seek past the last delivered key by appending &seek_after=<last_key> to the first GET on the new cursor, then continue iterating from where we left off. A second expiry on the same iterate() call raises CursorError.
    4. When next_key is null/absent, close the cursor and return.
    5. Deduplicate rows by key: if a row's key was already yielded in this iterate() call, skip it (at-least-once delivery with client-side dedupe).
    6. Always close the cursor on completion or unhandled exception.

CursorError: raised when the server cursor expires more than once in a single iterate() call.
